title: Closing Entries — The Full Sequence
event: At year-end, Ortiz Co. shows Service Revenue of $50,000, Salaries Expense and Rent Expense, and Dividends of $5,000. Prepare the four closing entries.

lines.label: zero out revenue
lines.why: Debit the revenue account against its credit balance to empty it.
lines.trap: Closing means ZEROING — temporary accounts start every year at zero.

lines.label: revenues pour in
lines.why: Revenues collect in the summary bucket.
lines.trap: Income Summary exists for about five minutes a year — only during closing.

lines.why: All expenses gather in the summary.
lines.trap: One combined debit — don't close expenses one entry at a time on an exam; it burns the clock.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Credit an expense to close it — the one time expenses take credits.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Same move — every expense account gets its own credit line.

lines.label: 50,000 − 38,000
lines.why: The summary holds a 12,000 credit balance — the year's net income.
lines.trap: The summary's balance IS net income. If it doesn't match the income statement, something upstream is wrong.

lines.label: income banked
lines.why: Income lands in retained earnings, permanently.
lines.trap: Credit RE for income — the direction flips in the loss variant.

lines.label: dividends out of RE
lines.trap: Dividends NEVER route through Income Summary — they aren't an expense.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Fourth and final closing entry, every year, income or loss.

lines.label: zero out revenue
lines.why: Same first step regardless of the year's result.
lines.trap: Steps 1, 2, and 4 are identical in income and loss years — only step 3 flips.

lines.label: revenues in
lines.trap: Nothing about a loss year changes how revenues close.

lines.why: Expenses total more than revenue this time.
lines.trap: The summary now holds a DEBIT balance — that's the loss signal.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Same mechanics as the income case.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Same mechanics as the income case.

lines.label: loss absorbed
lines.why: The loss comes OUT of retained earnings.
lines.trap: The direction flips with a loss — RE takes the debit, Income Summary takes the credit.

lines.label: 56,000 − 50,000 cleared
lines.trap: Whichever side the summary's balance sits on, the closing entry hits the OPPOSITE side.

lines.label: dividends out of RE
lines.why: Dividends still close straight to RE — loss year or not.
lines.trap: A loss year doesn't suspend the dividends close; RE just takes two debits this year.

lines.label: zeroed
lines.trap: Dividends and Income Summary never meet — in any year.

memorize.body: Closing order: REID — Revenues, Expenses, Income summary, Dividends.
Only TEMPORARY accounts close: revenues, expenses, dividends. Assets, liabilities, equity carry forward.
Income Summary's balance IS net income or loss. Credit balance → income → credit RE. Debit balance → loss → debit RE.

questions.prompt: Revenues $50,000, expenses $38,000, dividends $5,000. What amount is closed FROM Income Summary TO Retained Earnings?

distractors.feedback: You ran dividends through Income Summary. They close separately, straight against RE — the summary transfers only the 12,000 income.
That's gross revenue. Income Summary nets revenues against expenses first; only the 12,000 residual moves to RE.

build.scaffold: Four entries, REID order. Entry 3's direction depends on whether the summary holds a credit (income) or debit (loss).
